# Figure captions (draft) — generated by `openweight/make_figures.py`
PNG (preview) + PDF (vector, for LaTeX) in this folder. Regenerate after any data change.

## Fig. 1 — `fig1_crossmodel_fcr`
**Strict EC drives the false-confirmation rate to ≤3.4% across twelve models and five vendors.**
False-confirmation rate (FCR = false confirmations / 29 non-Delay cases) under the
Extract-then-Decide baseline (ED) versus Strict EC, for six open-weight models (left) and six
proprietary API models (right). The dashed line marks 3.4% (1/29). On every model that
over-commits at baseline, Strict EC reduces FCR to the floor; all six proprietary frontier
models converge to 3.4%.

## Fig. 2 — `fig2_hydraulic_effect`
**The Hydraulic Effect: a conserved, tunable commitment–abstention trade-off.**
Each model contributes three operating points — ED (●), Strict EC (■), Normalcy EC (▲) —
in the abstention–FCR plane. Blue arrows (ED→Strict) move down-and-right (more abstention,
fewer false confirmations); orange arrows (ED→Normalcy) move up-and-left (less abstention).
The consistent, sign-reversing displacement across all twelve models shows the stance acts as
a single externally controllable knob rather than a model-specific quirk.

## Fig. 3 — `fig3_cost_sensitivity`
**Cost-sensitive operating point (gpt-5.5): Strict EC is optimal once false confirmations are
costly.** Expected decision cost vs. the cost of a false confirmation (abstention fixed at 1,
false denial at 3). Strict EC (blue) has the lowest expected cost across essentially the whole
range; ED (red) and Self-Consistency (gray, overlapping ED — SC does not reduce FCR) are
worst as the false-confirmation cost grows. Pattern holds on 10/12 models (see
`COST_SENSITIVE_AND_COMPUTE.md`).

## Fig. 4 — `fig4_realdoc_granularity`
**Real documents: Strict EC's conservatism is evidence-calibrated, not reckless over-abstention.**
On naturally-occurring text from the source reports, evaluated at two granularities.
(a) Strict EC abstention falls from ~90% on isolated paragraphs (red) to 35–49% on
document-level contexts (blue). (b) On document-confirmed delays, Strict EC's detection rate
rises from 7–12% (fragments) to 76–88% (documents), with 0–6% false-denial. EC abstains when
evidence is genuinely insufficient and commits when it suffices — its conservatism is driven
by evidence sufficiency, not fragment length.
